questions.prompt: Complete: "I've already decided. I ___ study English tonight."
questions.explanation: A plan decided before now uses be going to + base verb: I am going to study tonight. "will to" is wrong (will never takes to), "going to" alone drops the be (am), and "will going to" stacks two futures together.

questions.prompt: The phone is ringing. You decide right now to answer it. What do you say?
questions.explanation: A decision made at the moment of speaking uses will: I'll get it. "I'm going to" suggests a plan you already had, the present "I get it" doesn't point to the future, and "will to get" wrongly adds to after will.

questions.prompt: Complete the prediction from evidence: "Look at those dark clouds! It ___ rain."
questions.explanation: A prediction based on evidence you can see uses be going to: it is going to rain. "is going" is missing to, "going to" is missing the be (is), and "will to" is never correct because will takes no to.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: "Don't worry, I ___ forget your birthday."
questions.explanation: The negative of will is won't (= will not): I won't forget. "willn't" is not an English word, "amn't" belongs to be (and there is no such form anyway), and "don't will" mixes two helpers.

questions.prompt: Make a question: "___ you going to visit your cousins next week?"
questions.explanation: A going-to question inverts be: with you the form is Are you going to…? "Is" is for he/she/it, "Do" is the present-simple helper (not used with going to), and "Will" would start a different structure (Will you visit…?).
